question.text: A scheduled commercial bank acquires shares of a listed company in the ordinary course of its lending business. Under what condition is this acquisition exempt from triggering an open offer?

answer.text: The bank must sell the shares within 6 months
The bank must not exercise voting rights beyond 1 year from the date of acquisition
The bank must not acquire more than 10% of the target company
The bank must obtain prior SEBI approval for each acquisition

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Acquisitions by scheduled commercial banks or financial institutions in the ordinary course of business are exempt from open offer, provided the voting rights on such shares are not exercised beyond 1 year from the date of acquisition. This balances commercial lending needs with takeover protection. **The Trap:** "Must sell within 6 months" sounds plausible but is not the stated condition — the regulation focuses on voting rights, not disposal timelines. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series IX (Merchant Banking) and banking-related certifications test the intersection of banking operations and securities regulations. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Banks that inadvertently exercise voting rights beyond the 1-year window lose the exemption retroactively — compliance teams must track these deadlines rigorously.
